retrieved_docs.title: Imdevimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Imdevimab is a recombinant neutralizing human immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal antibody to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. It binds to the S1 subunit of the spike protein receptor-binding domain (RBD), blocking the attachment of SARS-CoV-2 to the human ACE2 receptor. This prevents viral binding to the host cell, thereby preventing entry and replication of the virus, thus decreasing the viral load. Casirivimab binds to a non-overlapping portion of the spike protein RBD, similar to imdevimab, and the combination is theorized to limit the development of viral mutations. The cocktail of the 2 drugs was also found to have retained activity against the Alpha (B.1.1.7; UK), Beta (B.1.351; South Africa), Gamma (P.1; Brazil), Delta (B.1.617.2; India), Epsilon (B.1.427/429; California), Iota (B.1.526; New York), and Kappa (B.1.617.1; India) variants of SARS-CoV-2. The combination drug has no activity to the Omicron variant. [5] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Function -- Antiviral Activity of Baricitinib
retrieved_docs.content: The potential antiviral activity of Baricitinib is embedded in the mechanism of viral cell entry of several viruses including SARS-CoV-2. The virus gains entry into the cells by a process known as clathrin-mediated endocytosis [12] . Clathrin is a protein that is found in abundance on the inner surface of the cell membrane, this protein is involved in making the vesicle-like covering around the viral particle leading to endocytosis/entry into the cell. The first step in the process involves the binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the host cell surface receptor / ACE 2. This leads to the activation of 2 kinases, the AP2-associated protein kinase 1 (AAK-1) as well as the cyclin D-associated kinase (GAK). These two kinases namely the AAK-1 and the GAK lead to the activation of host proteins called the adaptor protein complexes (APs). These APs recruit clathrin to form the cage-like structure around the virus leading to endocytosis. Baricitinib has been shown to inhibit AAK-1, and this has been the basis of the proposed antiviral action [13] . This mechanism of action was suggested by a London-based machine learning study (Benevolent) that evaluated a large repository of approved medications and found potential inhibitory activity at clinically achievable serum concentrations. Baricitinib uniquely showed a high affinity for AAK-1 in comparison to other JAK inhibitors like Tofacitinib and Upadacitinib [14] . It should be noted that SARS-CoV-2 utilizes several endocytotic pathways for viral entry and this benefit could easily be circumvented with viral utilization of an alternate endocytotic pathway [15] . Image 2 depicts the proposed antiviral action of Baricitinib.

retrieved_docs.title: Casirivimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Casirivimab is a recombinant neutralizing human immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal antibody to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. It binds to the S1 subunit of the spike protein receptor-binding domain (RBD), blocking the attachment of SARS-CoV-2 to the human ACE2 receptor. This prevents viral binding to the host cell, thereby preventing entry and replication of the virus, thus decreasing the viral load. Imdevimab binds to a non-overlapping portion of the spike protein RBD, similar to casirivimab, and the combination is theorized to limit the development of viral mutations. The cocktail of the 2 drugs was also found to have retained activity against the Alpha (B.1.1.7; UK), Beta (B.1.351; South Africa), Gamma (P.1; Brazil), Delta (B.1.617.2; India), Epsilon (B.1.427/429; California), Iota (B.1.526; New York), and Kappa (B.1.617.1; India) variants of SARS-CoV-2. The combination drug has no activity to the Omicron variant. [5] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study evaluating the effect of remdesivir, nirmatrelvir, and molnupiravir reported that these three antiviral drugs might have therapeutic value against the Omicron subvariants BA.2.12.1, BA.4, and BA.5. [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir. This is an oral medication that consists of protease inhibitors. It has been shown to reduce hospitalization and death when given to high-risk, unvaccinated, non-hospitalized patients. It must be given within 5 days of symptoms onset. Of note, this medication has strong cytochrome P450 inhibition and therefore has many drug-drug interactions that need to be carefully assessed. Remdesivir. This is a nucleotide analog that inhibits the SARS-CoV-2 RNA polymerase. The recommended duration of therapy in this setting is 3 days. Molnupiravir. This is a mutagenic ribonucleoside antiviral agent. Due to the risk of genotoxicity with this agent, it is not recommended in pregnant patients. This agent should only be used if both of the above therapies are unavailable or cannot be given.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: At the onset of this pandemic, there was an urgency to mitigate this new viral illness with experimental therapies and drug repurposing. Since then, significant progress has been made in the management of COVID-19 due to the intense clinical research efforts globally that have resulted in novel therapeutics and vaccine development at an unprecedented speed. Currently, a variety of therapeutic options are available that include antiviral medications (e.g., molnupiravir, ritonavir in combination with nirmatrelvir, remdesivir), anti-SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibodies (e.g., bamlanivimab/etesevimab, casirivimab/imdevimab, sotrovimab, bebtelovimab), anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g., dexamethasone), immunomodulators agents (e.g., baricitinib, tocilizumab) are available under EUA for the management of COVID-19. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management -- Anti-SARS-CoV-2 Neutralizing Antibody Products
retrieved_docs.content: Previously, multiple monoclonal antibodies suggest B amlanivimab/Etesevimab, Sotrovimab, Casirivimab/Imdevimab and Beptelovimab had received FDA authorization for in the clinical management of COVID-19.  However, due to the emergence and dominance of Omicron subvariants, there are currently no monoclonal antibodies authorized to treat COVID-19.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management -- Antiviral Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Molnupiravir (named after the Norse god Thor's hammer Mjölnir) is a directly acting broad-spectrum oral antiviral agent acting on the RdRp enzyme was initially developed as a possible antiviral treatment for influenza, alphaviruses including Eastern, Western, and Venezuelan equine encephalitic viruses. Based on a meta-analysis of available phase 1-3 studies, molnupiravir demonstrated a significant reduction in hospitalization and death in mild COVID-19 disease. [75]  Results from a phase 3 double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial reported that early treatment with molnupiravir reduced the risk of hospitalization or death in at-risk unvaccinated adults with mild-to-moderate, laboratory-confirmed COVID-19. [76]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention of COVID-19
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccination to prevent SARS-CoV-2 infection

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction -- The need for additional therapeutic agents
retrieved_docs.content: Over the course of the past year, our understanding of the pathophysiology of COVID-19 has improved. With ongoing randomized controlled trials evaluating therapies for COVID -19, like the RECOVERY Trial recruiting over 39,000 individuals, the guidelines for therapy for COVID-19 remain fluid. Despite a better understanding of the disease, the mortality and morbidity associated with COVID-19 remains unacceptably high.  The recent wave of overwhelming cases in the Republic of India is a grave example of the potential for disaster with COVID-19. There is a need for agents that can be added to the existing arsenal of approved medications. An ideal therapeutic agent should be reliable with robust clinical benefits. The recommendation for its use should be backed by evidence, and it should have a favorable and benign side effect profile. Apart from this, the agent should be readily available to the masses, should be inexpensive, and should be able to meet an exorbitant demand with an efficient supply chain. In an attempt to apply these criteria through this review; we evaluate the evidence for and against the concurrent use of Remdesivir and Baricitinib, indications and contraindications, mechanism of action, and the side effect profile of these drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ritonavir, the second component, is a human immunodeficiency virus-1 (HIV-1) protease inhibitor with CYP3A-inhibiting effects. The latter effect allows it to be used as a pharmacokinetic boosting agent to decrease the CYP3A-mediated metabolism of nirmatrelvir. Coadministration of nirmatrelvir with ritonavir is needed to increase the plasma concentration of nirmatrelvir enough to achieve the targeted therapeutic range. [15] Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is effective against major variants of SARS-CoV-2, including omicron variants. [16] The proposed mechanism of resistance is the possibility of potential escape mutations. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the NIH guidelines, individuals with mild illness can be managed in the ambulatory setting with supportive care and isolation. Laboratory and radiographic evaluations are routinely not indicated. Elderly patients and those with pre-existing conditions should be monitored closely until clinical recovery is achieved. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) Covid-19 treatment guidelines panel recommends the use of Paxlovid or Remdesivir in order of preference  as preferred therapies for outpatients at high risk of disease progression with a low threshold to consider hospitalization for closer monitoring. The panel recommends clinical use of alternative therapies such as Molnupiravir ONLY if there is non-availability of preferred therapies, not feasible to use, or clinically inappropriate. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) Covid-19 Treatment Guidelines Panel recommends against dexamethasone in mild illness. Moderate Illness

retrieved_docs.title: Features, Evaluation, and Treatment of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir is the only antiviral drug approved by the USA Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat COVID-19. Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir, molnupiravir, and high-titer COVID-19 convalescent plasma have Emergency Use Authorizations (EUAs) for treating COVID-19. Tixagevimab 300 mg plus cilgavimab 300 mg monoclonal antibodies have received EUAs that allow them to be used as SARS-CoV-2 preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) in certain patients. Many other monoclonal antibodies had EUAs; however, as Omicron subvariants emerged, their EUAs were revoked as they were no longer effective. The most recent NIH treatment guidelines for the management of COVID-19 illness (accessed on January 3rd, 2023) are outlined below:[ NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines ]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir  is an oral combination pill of two antiviral agents which, on an interim analysis of phase 2-3 data (reported via press release), which included 1219 patients, found that the risk of COVID-19-related hospital admission or all-cause mortality was 89% lower in the ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir group when compared to placebo when started within three days of symptom onset. Further studies are ongoing to establish the efficacy reported. [77] On 22 December 2021, the FDA issued a EUA authorizing the use of ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir for patients with mild to moderate COVID-19.

retrieved_docs.title: Casirivimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Casirivimab is a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against spike protein (S) of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) that causes coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). It is 1 of the SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibodies proposed for use in the clinical management of COVID-19. [1] On 21st November 2020, the combination of casirivimab and imdevimab was authorized for emergency use by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA). These are investigational drugs not approved for any specific indication. FDA allowed these drugs only to be administered together. Casirivimab (REGN10933) and imdevimab (REGN10987) are also known as REGN-COV2 or Regeneron. The combination drug has been shown to decrease viral load  and decrease the risk of hospitalization and emergency visits. REGN-COV2, administered prophylactically or therapeutically, has prevented COVID-19-induced sequelae in non-human primates. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir may be used to treat عدوى فيروسية, offering therapeutic benefit for patients with viral infections, whereas ribavirin may be employed to prevent عدوى فيروسية, serving as a prophylactic antiviral option. Both agents are relevant in the clinical management of viral diseases, with remdesivir focusing on treatment and ribavirin on prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is an antiretroviral medication that functions as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it acts as a protease inhibitor that blocks the activity of viral proteases essential for HIV replication, highlighting its therapeutic role in managing HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2775
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is identified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 CYP3A enzyme, a property that underlies its contraindication when used together with ritonavir. The interaction between amiodaron and ritonavir is classified as a contraindicated drug class combination, reflecting the risk of adverse effects due to CYP3A‑mediated drug‑drug interactions. Both amiodaron and ritonavir are listed as concepts within the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology, indicating that each agent is recognized in the context of COVID‑19–related pharmacotherapy. Understanding the inhibitory effect of amiodaron on CYP3A and its contraindication with ritonavir is essential for safe medication management, especially when these agents are considered for patients with COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 607
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 metabolizes miconazole, omeprazole and amiodarone; amiodarone is also a substrate of CYP3A4. Both miconazole and amiodarone inhibit the CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes, creating potential drug‑drug interactions when co‑administered with other CYP3A4‑metabolized agents. Amiodarone is specifically contraindicated with ritonavir, highlighting a clinically important interaction that must be avoided. The NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology and the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology each list amiodarone, clindamycin, calcifediol, omeprazole, ritonavir, zinc sulfate (síran zinečnatý) and ketotifen as concepts, indicating their relevance to COVID‑19 research, treatment guidelines or therapeutic investigations. Ketotifen is classified as an antipruritic and appears in both COVID‑19 terminologies; it is a constituent of the pharmaceutical preparation identified as Preparation K. Inclusion of these agents—particularly the CYP‑interacting drugs miconazole, amiodarone and omeprazole—within COVID‑19‑specific vocabularies underscores the need for careful prescribing and interaction management in patients receiving COVID‑19‑related therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 320
retrieved_docs.content: The medicated shampoo MiconaHex + Triz contains chlorhexidine gluconate and the imidazole‑derived systemic antifungal miconazole nitrate; miconazole is metabolized by human cytochrome P450 3A4 and acts as an inhibitor of CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes. Human CYP3A4 also clears the chemotherapeutic agent paclitaxel, illustrating the enzyme’s broad role in drug metabolism. Amiodarone, an antiarrhythmic listed in both the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, is similarly metabolized by CYP3A4 and inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A, leading to contraindications with the antiretrovirals nelfinavir mesylate and ritonavir, which share these pathways. Clindamycin (recorded as “klindamycin”) and the vitamin D analog kalcifediol are also included in the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology, underscoring their relevance to COVID‑19 therapeutic vocabularies. Aspirin appears across the NCIt Antineoplastic Agent Terminology, the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology, and the NCIt COVID‑19

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 467
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) metabolizes mikonazol, amiodarone, and omeprazole, while amiodarone and mikonazol both inhibit CYP2C9 and CYP3A, creating potential drug‑drug interactions for agents that rely on these pathways. Amiodarone is an anti‑arrhythmic used in cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation but is contraindicated with ritonavir because ritonavir strongly inhibits CYP3A4, raising amiodarone levels and toxicity risk. Acebutolol, a beta‑blocker, is also capable of treating cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, whereas digoxin is contraindicated in these life‑threatening arrhythmias. The NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology list several agents relevant to COVID‑19 management, including aspirin, clindamycin, kalcifediol, omeprazole, ritonavir, zinc sulfate, and dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA, classified in the ATC system as an other sex hormone/modulator of the genital system). Aspirin frequently co‑occurs with ranitidine and digoxin, and digoxin also co‑occurs with ranitidine, reflecting common prescribing patterns for cardiovascular conditions and gastrointestinal protection. Collectively, these metabolic relationships, inhibitory effects, contraindications, therapeutic uses, and co‑prescription patterns underscore the necessity of careful medication management when drugs share CYP450 pathways or are employed in complex clinical scenarios such as COVID‑19 infection and acute cardiac emergencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 418
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base links several dermatologic, cardiovascular, antiviral, and metabolic agents through their pharmacologic properties, enzymatic interactions, and relevance to COVID‑19 therapeutics. The medicated shampoos marketed as chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL (VetraSeb) and a related chlorhexidine‑miconazole formulation contain miconazole as the active moiety; miconazole is metabolized by human cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) and acts as an inhibitor of CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes. Amiodarone (amiodaron) is also a CYP3A4 substrate, inhibits CYP2C9, CYP3A, and additional enzymes, and is contraindicated with ritonavir; it is used in cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation. Atenolol is similarly noted for treating cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation. Omeprazole (omeprazol) is a CYP3A4 substrate and enzyme inhibitor, and together with amiodarone, kalcifediol, and ritonavir it appears in the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, indicating a role in emerging COVID‑19 treatment frameworks. Zinc sulfate (síran zinečnatý) is also included in these COVID‑19 vocabularies, underscoring its therapeutic relevance. In the enzyme‑inhibition hierarchy, the class “inhibitory enzymů” (inhibitory enzymes) is a child of “inhibitory syntézy proteinů” (inhibitory protein synthesis) and is related to protein C; honokiol is classified as an instance of the inhibitory enzymes class, reflecting its function as an enzyme inhibitor. Collectively, these relationships emphasize the central role of CYP3A4 in drug metabolism, the potential for drug‑drug interactions when CYP2C9 or CYP3A inhibitors are co‑administered, and the intersection of dermatologic agents, cardiovascular drugs, antiviral therapies, and enzyme‑inhibition pathways in both routine patient care and COVID‑19‑related research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: antiviral therapy (e.g., telaprevir). Together, these entities illustrate a comprehensive therapeutic landscape that integrates hierarchical classification, mechanistic specificity, drug‑interaction considerations, diagnostic imaging agents, and a spectrum of antiviral modalities—from nucleoside analogues and entry inhibitors to monoclonal antibodies—each with defined indications and roles essential for optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: ibalizumab, bulevirtide, fostemsavir; • Other agents – moroxydine, tiloron, tecovirimat, brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir. NRTIs act as nucleoside analogues that terminate HIV reverse transcription, whereas non‑RTI nucleoside‑like agents (brincidofovir, brivudine) inhibit viral DNA polymerases and remdesivir targets the RNA‑dependent RNA polymerase of SARS‑CoV‑2. Bulevirtide, enfuvirtide, fostemsavir and ibalizumab block viral entry; tecovirimat and tiloron impede viral egress; moroxydine interferes with viral DNA replication. Lipopeptide chemistry links bulevirtide and the echinocandin antifungal caspofungin, the latter together with micafungin inhibiting β‑(1,3)‑D‑glucan synthesis in fungal cell walls. Monoclonal antibodies ibalizumab and abciximab expand therapeutic options beyond small molecules. Adjunctive agents include the antibiotic aztreonam (1000 mg injectable) and the cholesterol‑binding resin cholestyramine (4000 mg oral suspension). Pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2877
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Agent Terminology includes the agents kalcifediol and síran zinečnatý (zinc sulfate). Kalcifediol, a metabolite of vitamin D, has been investigated for its potential immunomodulatory and anti‑inflammatory effects in the context of COVID‑19. Síran zinečnatý, a zinc salt, provides elemental zinc, an essential trace element that supports antiviral immunity and may help reduce viral replication. By cataloguing these specific agents, the NCIt COVID-19 Agent Terminology facilitates organized classification, research, and clinical consideration of therapeutic options relevant to COVID‑19 management.
